title: 2025/12/03 - FEC (Part 3)
displayTitle: 2025-12-03 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Inland Revenue Department [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Inland Revenue Department
description: Wednesday 3 December 2025 | Finance and Expenditure Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Inland Revenue Department Hearing of evidence: Inland Revenue Department Peter Mersi, Commissioner of Inland Revenue David Carrigan, Deputy Commissioner, Policy James Grayson, Deputy Commissioner, Customer & Compliance Services – Individuals Lisa Barrett, Deputy Commissioner, Customer & Compliance Services – Business Nick Bradley, Enterprise Leader, Finance Services
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Inland Revenue Department. Inland Revenue Department. Peter Mersi, Commissioner of Inland Revenue

chunks.text: Start another round, uh, Dr. Russell. So, um, first of all, I would just like to thank Inland revenue for page 16 of the report where you have restored the information. It wasn't there last year, but the rest about where we have, where we've spent money, because I did miss that last year and I really appreciate having it back this year.

And I will social media it later. Thank You. And, and in retrospect, I think it's a good idea that we do share what the money is used for. So thank You for the prompt. That's the easiest place to find our government account. Um, investment boost or the immediate, um, we, we do have a real set of concerns around that.

Um, so it started, what was the budget date this year? That was the date that 22nd of that. Um, I don't know how many people have claimed that accelerated. So that immediate write off yet? Not yet, no. Do you have systems in place for tracking it? Uh, so, um, we, we have put, we're in the process of putting a, uh, a data point in the IR 10 return Right.

For next, for the, you know, the next return. So, um, sorry For the tax year ending 31 March, 2020. Yeah. Yep, yep. So, um, that will record if people have claimed the investment boost. Yeah. So we'll know how much has been claimed. Yep. Um, the way our tax returns work though is, is we won't receive that until July, 2027 because, because people had so long to file.

Yep. So, but yeah, we, we, we are in the process of Flexing that, but eventually we will get some data on how many people have Yep. How many entities have claimed it, and how much they've claimed. Yep. Great. Okay. Um, so we're just have to wait for that information. Um, but in the development of, uh, sorry, that policy, um, one of the things we were concerned about was the commercial buildings and the fact that people had already started on commercial buildings then get a bonus.

Yeah. Uh, what sort of policy work did you do around that? So it's a, it's a, it's a really good question. So if you think about the concept for investment boost, um, it was designed to apply to new marginal investment, not, not investment. That's, that's already there. Yeah.

So with a building, obviously it's got a long lead time. So we did have commercial buildings that were in the process of being built that spanned the budget. So we had a choice. We could either go do the Australian approach, which is essentially provide the investment boost from when, when the contracts were entered into, essentially.

Yeah. So in other words, those wouldn't qualify because the contracts were entered into before the application date. Yeah. Or the other approach, and this is the one we took, um, was to allow investment boost for assets where they become available for use after the application date.

And the reason for that is because we've got to get these rules, the investment boost rules to merge well with the depreciation rules. Yep. And depreciation turns on on availability for use. Yep. So that's why we, but, but we do accept that you, you'd end up providing investment booths to buildings that had already been started.

So that's the, that's the explanation. Yeah. So The alternative in terms of, I mean that I, I get that that's Yeah. Makes sense. But the alternative would've been to exclude commercial buildings. Um, you Know, you, you could exclude commercial buildings. Um, our, our, our strong advice to the government was if, if you look at the effective marginal tax rates on different forms of investment, the ones that are the highest are ones which involve commercial buildings.

So, and that's because we don't allow depreciation on them. So what we were saying was, well, our advice was actually reinstate depreciation on commercial buildings and provide investment boost. Right. But they, the, the decision was made to, to, to do investment boost.

So that, that was our advice. They, that it just, so, um, and, and that's because of the effective margin tax rates on commercial buildings. Try trying to, try and trying to remain neutral as to encouraging what's, you know, what sort of investment basically. So a commercial building gets the 20% immediate write off, but no further depreciation. That's right. Okay. Alright.

Well, we're just have to wait and see on the information on that. Um, I, I, um, Dr. Ross, I should also say we are doing a survey as well. So, um, in December, I think we're putting out a survey, which is which, because we realize the tax returns is gonna take so long. Yeah. Which will ask people, um, businesses, um, you know, have you responded to this investment boost, and if so, how?

Right. Oh, if so, How So, so, so in other words, has this investment boost, um, what we're trying to get at there is have you invested in things that you otherwise wouldn't have invested into? So, ha has, has your behavior changed because of investment boost, which That's gonna be hard to get in a survey. So, Which the only way really is to get I know, I Know. Yeah. Are you gonna get people to respond as swap classes of assets they bought? Or just that broad Question? Yeah, so, so we haven't completely finished designing it

yet, but it's, um, it will ask classes of assets and is there something that you, you wouldn't have bought if it wasn't for investment boost? So it's quite right. So, and itself, it'll be people reporting their own behaviors, But yeah, it's not, I mean, it's not perfect, but it'll Give us a broad brush. Okay. Thank you. Yep. Okay. Supplementary Dr. Woods.

What, uh, we had a, a conversation with treasury this morning about this as well in terms of the advice that went in to developing the, this, the, the policy. And so 1% growth in GDP over 20 year period, it's quite a gradual increase in GDP.

Um, but one of the things that we're really interested in knowing is around the, the inputs into the methodology to get to the, the one per the 1% over 20 year figure, um, is this dependent on particular, uh, ratios of classes of a assets, for example. So, uh, plant, um, versus Utes.

Um, so in terms of, so any information that we can get around where you think if we're gonna maximize the GDP growth, we need to see the highest levels of investment. So what, just to explain how we did the methodology just in the high level. So what we did is, the first thing we did is, is we looked at all the different classes of assets.

So, um, we looked at, um, The four big categories, buildings, construction, plum, machinery and transport. Which, which is essentially the, uh, and what we did is we, we, we used a model that we used for our long-term insights briefing a few years ago and measured the current effective tax rates on all of those things.

Mm-hmm. Um, and we found that they were on some of 'em, they were quite high. And then what we did is we took, we modeled the investment boost and we said, how much would investment boost lower those effective tax rates across all those asset categories? And then what we did is we took that input and we put it into, um, a model that a US that we got from a US academic called Jane Gravel.

And that modeled, that was an, that was the input into that model. And we obviously made a whole bunch of other assumptions in that model. And that was the model that generated the increase over 20 years for GDP of 1% and wages of one point half percent. And it also modeled that you'll get half of that increase in the first five years I think.

So that, that was our methodology. Um, and we, we, yeah. So anyway, that's, that's just That, just just on that so that, so, so half a percent growth in GDP in the first five years, and that's, that's what The money Five in wages. Yeah. And we're not going to really have much of a sense of how that's performing until 2027 in terms of those Other than the survey results, than the survey, which might give us in an early indication. Yeah.

So in terms of the, the tracking of the performance of, of, of that modeling, it's gonna be very difficult to do, isn't it? Yeah. It's just the nature of the tax system though, that that drives that outcome rather than Totally, yeah. Um, did you provide any advice, um, or alternative models with caps, sun sunset clauses targeting?

I mean, this is, as I understand it, an open-ended appropriation, um, that you could have some very big projects, um, that come in and, and file for some very large, um, amounts. Um, and in fact, ministers themselves were having to check with colleagues sitting in the chair when it was on the floor of the house, whether or not, I mean, minister Jones was checking whether or not a new oil and gas field, for example, would qualify.

Um, did you provide any, yeah, so any advice around that? So we, our, our strong advice to, because the, the policy rationale was an inc an increasing capital stock across the board. So the, the, the, the logic go goes something along the lines of New Zealand as capital shallow, we want a policy lever that will increase capital stock across the board, what we said as inland revenue and and treasury.

Um, we don't think it's, we think the market is best placed to work out where those, where that capital stock should increase, should occur. So our, our strong advice was to, was to apply the, um, investment boosts to all assets, including commercial buildings except for intellectual property, because it's that, um, that, that guess gives you some integrity concerns.

Um, and that was broadly accepted. A cap was discussed. Our gain, our strong advice was do not have a cap. What we're interested in here is an increase in capital stock. We want big investment and small investment. We shouldn't be discriminating against big investment. So, um, that was, that was our, um, that was our advice, and it was, it was accepted.

So that's, yeah. Importantly, it's based on the objectives That were set by. Yeah. Okay. Dan Butler. Thanks. Were there any modeling around different, um, rates? You know, we, we landed on the 20%. Were they modeling, you know, 40% percent as a Yeah. Can you tell us? Yeah, I, I, I, I'm sure we can come back to you on exactly what we did provide different scenarios.

There was like a, I'm pretty sure there was like a, a 10, a 20, 40, maybe even gone up to 50. Um, so we did, we provide all those models and the general conclusion was 20% was a bit of a sweet spot. Um, but I think the model does tell you that you can go up to 40% and, and while the gains do diminish a bit, you still get net gains.

Yeah. And then it just becomes can you afford to, yeah. Yeah. After 40, you start to subsidize the tax system starts to subsidize investments. That would never have happened if it wasn't for the tax system. Yeah, understood. So, but I can give you a bit more on, oh, I think, is that about, is that enough? Yeah, that's enough. Yeah. Thank you. Okay.

Um, primary from Chloe Sw, uh, are you guys familiar with tech, just tax justice, ros, uh, big tech little tax report? I am. Um, no. Cool. Um, so I guess just then to step through a bit of it, um, when the government made the decision not to pursue the digital services tax, did you guys end up providing any advice about alternatives that could possibly be pursued?

Or was that, that, So on the digital services tax, um, we, our, our advice on that has always been a multilateral option is better than a unilateral option. Mm-hmm. Because unilateral options are, are messy. Every country has a different one. Um, but so we've always provided that advice.

Um, when the government decided to not continue with the digital service and take it off the order paper, we didn't provide any additional advice on whether there should be an alternative to that. Mm-hmm. Does it concern the IRD that these massive multi, multi, multi-billion dollar corporations, multinational corporations, are currently making billions of dollars in this country and paying very little in tax?

Well, that's, it's, um, I Mean, that's why we're in favor of the multilateral approach. Mm-hmm. Um, you know, we continue to be engaged at the OECD. Mm-hmm. Uh, in the hope of, um, bringing a resolution that is, that is multilateral in nature and provides, you know, an appropriate set of outcomes for New Zealand.

Okay. Um, to that effect, have you been providing, so I'm hearing that you've got a work stream or are engaged in work at the OECD level? Yeah. Yeah, that's, that's Right. Yeah. And has there been any further information commissioned or advice commissioned by ministers on something along the lines of dealing with this tax treatment of these No, the, the moment we're very much, you know, there's still, there, there are conversations going on at the LECD.

So pillar one is the, is the multilateral solution that would be instead of a digital services tax mm-hmm. Um, that has, that really, I mean, while work is still happening, it has essentially stall mm-hmm.

Um, the, there's a lot of action happening on the pillar two one, which is basically around ensuring or trying to ensure that countries have incentives to have at least a 15% company tax tax rate. Mm-hmm. And that is, um, that is, you know, we, there, there are discussions going on there, but that's probably where more of the action is at the moment. Mm-hmm.

Um, one of the proposals or ideas in that tax report is applying legislation that we already have in the form of the 5% withholding tax rates stipulated, obviously in the New Zealand US double taxation agreement to service licensing fees for the big four being Google, uh, Facebook, Amazon, and Microsoft. And they estimate that this could yield around $130 million per annum.

Does that track at all with any work that you've done? Um, I mean, I, I, I understand the proposal. Um, I, I just, I just need to take a look at it. Um, yeah. But, um, I, I, I don't think we've actively looked at that. I mean, I think the, the, essentially the DST was mm-hmm.

Was the alternate to what was happening at the a CD, the pillar one. So yeah, I don't think we've, we've taken, we haven't had any further work Mm. On alternatives to That. I guess part of what I'm getting at here is that, you know, with enforcement, uh, and the other work that even taken in the past year or two, uh, you've been looking at how you apply already existing stuff to get a greater tax take from those who should be paying, uh, if we are to fairly and justly apply the laws that we already have as to whether those are fair and just

as a matter of judgment, et cetera. But to that effect, I mean, why have you not been looking at this? And when it comes to these massive multinational tech corporations? So essentially at the moment with an existing legislation, they're paying what they're required to pay. I think what the OECD work does, what the DST would do is to change the nature of the legislation.

So, uh, so it is not like they're not actually, there's no suggestion that they are breaking the law or breaking the tax law. Um, and the focus on what the appropriate taxation for that group should be has been a, the preference for the multilateral approach.

Uh, but what I'm getting at here is that one of the proposals is just to apply existing legislation when it comes to the withholding tax rate

Yes, it was useful to have those powers. I think it depends on the question that you're asking, uh, as to whether those powers effectively were necessary, are necessary or not. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Uh, well, you don't need to collect it in a compulsory fashion. Because we put the draft out and we're currently going through the submissions. Um, but essentially it, it looked at the future and said, if you want a tax system that is sufficiently, uh, resilient to be able to respond to, um, future demands, uh, potentially increased revenue, then firstly, a tax system whose core structure is, um, broad based and stable with flexibility being driven

through changes in rates, provides the best, um, approach to resilience. And then looked at the range of choices you have in thinking about, um, what levers you could use in the future if you, if you looked at revenue as being one of the tools you wanted to use.

And it concludes that there are a broad range of things you can do. Everything from expanding, uh, the way in which, uh, capital gains tax is applied right through to adjustments to GST rates, but with a, um, a consequential sort of, uh, mechanism for ensuring low income people received an offset.

So sort of spanned a lot, it didn't conclude a preference for any of those options. That really just sets out what they are.
